title: AMBROSIA BEETLE SURFACE STERILIZATION
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"4ha5t","text":"Surface sterilization of large quantities of ambrosia beetles can become an arduous and time-consuming endeavor. Elizabeth Moore developed this simple and effective protocol to quickly surface sterilize large numbers of ambrosia beetles in preparation for experiments and in the events of contaminant fungi outbreaks, i.e. Trichoderma. Total time required is \u003c30 minutes for 20 up to hundreds of beetles.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":323,"length":11}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"b632d","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"96imk","text":"\nFor rearing purposes in bolts, this protocol may be carried out on the bench. For rearing in diet tubes or for experiments, use sterilized equipment and solutions in a biosafety cabinet.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
materials_text: {"blocks":[{"key":"5ivv3","text":"    • 0.01% TWEEN","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"emoqr","text":"    • 75% Ethanol","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8e40q","text":"    • 2% Bleach","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"261rs","text":"    • diH2O","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"64dek","text":"    • 5 beakers (250 mL is ideal)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"45gjp","text":"    • Soft forceps","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5d5e7","text":"    • Snap ball tea strainer","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"c8p4","text":"    • Funnel","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ai4jp","text":"    • Small clip","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"10m2b","text":"    • 50mL falcon tube","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7a3dt","text":"    • Paper towels or filter paper","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bkuqr","text":"    • Sterile petri dish","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"165qc","text":"    • Sterile filter paper","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Stage beetles in container while preparing for wash.
Prepare/add 75% Ethanol, 2% bleach, 0.01% tween in beakers and add diH2O to two beakers.
Use a funnel to add beetles to a falcon tube, tapping as necessary to prevent escapees.
The tea strainer can comfortably hold 50-100 beetles.
Remove funnel from tube and cover the top of the falcon tube with one side of the tea strainer.
Flip the tube, keep the end covered with the strainer, and tap the beetles out into the strainer.
Close the tea strainer and use a small clip to seal any gaps beetles could get out through.
Repeatedly dunk the tea strainer with beetles inside into the beakers for about ten seconds:
Dab the stainer to dry on a paper towel
Dump beetles into sterile petri dish with sterile filter paper to dry.

Be careful not to crush beetles in the strainer while removing them, use soft forceps to remove any beetles remaining in the strainer. Letting the strainer close may crush beetles and beetles may become stuck in water droplets within the strainer.
creator: M. Elizabeth Moore
